speaker: interviewer
text: Roll [INAUDIBLE]. OK, get focused. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't-- wait, hold on one sec. Do you have a, a grease pencil for that other? It's really hard to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it sure is.

speaker: interviewer
text: They always seem to do it with a grease pen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can read this real good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Get your mic on. And when you're verbal, slate this.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Tell me when.

speaker: interviewer
text: Scrolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date is August 15, 1995. The survivor is Boris Abel or Abelski. Interviewer, Sandy Jacobson, Los Angeles, California, USA. Interview will be in English. All right. Now I'm going to do the--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to do the same thing with him and mark now [? to CB. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to note that it's tape 1? Or is that-- that's all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's some sort of a given on this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we do tape 2 on the other ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Move in.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to sit with you for one second. I'm Sandy Jacobson. This is Boris Abel. The date is August 15, 1995. We'll be speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling. Any time you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Boris, thank you so much for participating in the project. Please tell me your name and the name you were born with.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is now Boris Abel. Before I was in Lithuanian, Berelis Abelskis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in 1915, July 23, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Panevezys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Panevezys is in Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother and father.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my father?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, the name and tell me about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- first-- my father's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is [BACKROUND NOISE Schneir Benjamin Abelskis. My mother's name is [BACKROUND NOISE] Sonya Abelskis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a rope and twine manufacturer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big company?

speaker: interviewee
text: He established this in 1902. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On-- after the war, [BACKROUND NOISE] when his children grew up, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was the company larger, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was larger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Larger. And after this, when I grew up, when I was [BACKROUND NOISE] maybe 18 years old, we made a corporation. And the family was the owners from the corporation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we had branches in the capital cities of Lithuania. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to import all kind stuff from all over the world. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] It's a big company.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you refer to after the war then the children were involved, you mean after World War I, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father come over because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war, he had to come in. And during the war, he was-- no, but when the revolution was, he was in in Russia. And after this, he come over back to Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had four brothers and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in the family? What number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you were almost the baby then, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was religious, yeah. Family was religious, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --remember-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was religious. He was-- he even built a synagogue by himself. And he was the president of the synagogue. And he was the cantor of the synagogue. And he was the treasurer of the synagogue. And he was the supporter of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a lot of them, but was a lot of synagogues. Although my father had to have, like, the Hasidic-- a Hasidic synagogue that-- he established this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. When you talk about your father, was your mother also religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father-- my mother was going according to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the whole family had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --go, you know, a little bit [LAUGHS] in the background. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- tell me about the holidays as you remember them. Which, which holidays stand out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: All, all holidays-- we observe all the holidays, especially the holidays where we used to be-- Easter and the High Holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover and the High Holidays, Yom Kippur and New Year, see? This holiday, when we grew up and my brothers and sisters was married with children, that we used to come in all together in one place in Panevezys. And we used to celebrate the holidays all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all our stores all over the country used to be closed. And they used to come in all together to be in one place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So that was a lot of fun for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was in this time as not a small kid. But I liked it, you know? We used to wait for that, you know, because it's not like today, you know. We used to go-- maybe two months before the holidays, my mother used to prepare everything, you know. [BACKROUND NOISE] You couldn't go in the grocer and just buy the stuff, you know. They, they used to make the-- everything-- prepared to have everything for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Close the door. Did she make matzah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matzah-- we bought matzah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to buy it. But this same-- a long time before holiday, we used to buy all the matzah should be-- for sure we have the matzah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in case they will run out. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Close the door. So did you-- now, tell me about the house you lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- the house was-- the-- our factory's a rope, rope factory. We had a lot of land, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to live in the-- in the same place where the factory is. And we had two houses there. And after this, we build that big house at [NON-ENGLISH], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the big house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same place, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like? Was it fancy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When, when we build the house, was flowing water, you know? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything what we got like today, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. That was unusual then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because not everybody had it, you know. Before even the-- before, people didn't even-- when we used to have a well, you know, for takes out water. We had two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole-- the whole streets used to come in. We used to give them the water free, you know, because not everybody could afford to have a well. Well, after this, when we-- was more modern, we built already with motors. We used to, from this well, used to go in the water in, in the kitchen, you know, in bathtub, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had, like, should be in 50 years later. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have electricity in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, electricity. We had all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a telephone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Telephone, we had, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you really had everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had because-- not everybody had, though. But we had-- first thing, we was in business. And the other thing is we was comfortable, you know, with, with finances.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father drive a car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't drive a car. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get around?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to [SIGHS] go around-- they used to have in the city, you know, like, taxis. They had a few, not too many. And they had horses with, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they call those-- horse and buggies--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --but then, like, for passenger, you know? And they used to call in. And they used to come in and take you. And we used to walk a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to walk a lot. [BACKROUND NOISE] And we used to have trains and subways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you had subways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this town is-- in this town, they didn't have a subway. But I was in all kinds cities. See, I had in Kovno. Kovno is the capital in this time. They had already subways, and they had buses, you know. You used to go each 5-10 minutes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people lived in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my town was living 35,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. Any idea of how many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: 9,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In public school, I didn't go because for Jewish people, it's hard to go in there. Maybe in the whole public school maybe was two, three people. Oh, we had-- the Jewish community had a-- a gymnasium they call this, a high school. And they had professional, beautiful teachers, you know, from Switzerland and from Germany, you know? And they supervised-- not supervised-- the Lithuanian government education--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --department used to control this, you know. They didn't support us. But they controlled us, you know. When they-- when we had the examens, they used to come in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, and check if everything goes right. And they had-- they used to even send out-- the controllers, they used to send out in Jerusalem to teach them the Hebrew language because everything was in Hebrew. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would send the non-Jewish controllers to Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Jerusalem in a-- in a university. And they used to graduate for the Hebrew language. And after this, they used to come in. They used to control the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Jewish gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very interesting. Do you remember your own bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember bar mitzvah, yeah. The bar--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bar mitzvah-- beside, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] beside in, in the gymnasium, they teach us religion, you know, the Bible, and the Tanakh, and all kind stuff. When-- on the end, after school, my father used to send me to a, a rabbi, a private rabbi, for teaching, you know, this Judaic study. And before bar mitzvah, they had a student-- we used to go to became a, a rabbi. They had special places where people go to became rabbis. And it's not like in United States, four years. They used to go there by 20 years. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used to teach me, you know-- Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --how to do the bar mitzvah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after your bar mitzvah, did you have a party?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just in the house. We didn't have in this time hotels, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. My father invited the, the synagogue's members, you know, who was in, in the synagogue in this Saturday. He invite in the house, and-- Did you have that-- your bar mitzvah-- in your father's synagogue? Did he have it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now which of your brothers were you closest to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was close to all of them, you know? But one thing is I was close to the youngest one, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one-- he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, he's now in Israel. He was in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel. So-- OK, now, do you remember if you were-- did you have any interaction with the non-Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We was-- before the war, we didn't have too much trouble between the Jews and non-Jews because the president [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of Lithuania was very good to Jewish people. He was elected president, but it's not like in United States. The mayors of the cities used to elect him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, but the only thing, when they established Lithuania in 1918 after the First World War, the United Nation declared all the countries was occupied by Russian, they used to be released-- that they released Lithuania, too. And they made a, a government there. And this president was President Antanas Smetona. He was there for six months. And after six months, somebody took over the government, like now in South America, you know. He took over the government. And he was, like, a little bit communism, you know, like a socialist, you know. He took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took it over. But he didn't do nothing to him. He released him, just took away the job from him when they took him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this man who they threw over, Smetona, he was acquainted with a Jewish-- two brothers. Their name was [NON-ENGLISH]. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was contractors. And they was comfortable people, rich people, and they knew him-- that when they threw him out from his position, they gave him a beautiful home. And they supported him with his family. And after a while, he took over the government back from this guy who took over from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he became president. And he was president all the time till the Russian come over in 1940 when they took over the country. And I saw even when he left-- my store was across from his palace. And I saw his family and cars, they left for the German border. And from-- Germany let him in because they had agreement with the Russian government when they took it over to let him out, you know. And they let them out. And he come over in Germany. And after from Germany, he left for United States. And he was living in Chicago. And in Chicago was a fire. And in fire, he was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] burned up in fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because of those Jews being nice to him, then he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was good to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was, yeah. When even he president, these Jews, these two, they helped him a lot. Well, this is not official. But we know he's-- they helped him in all kind advice, you know. And, and he was very nice to Jewish people. We used to even call them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. So in your town, you were fairly-- you felt pretty safe. You felt secure, mostly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- well, in his-- in our town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the president's wife's sister [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was the wife from our mayor from the city where I was to-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good place to be, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how I remember this. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Did you have friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why do-- I remember things when I was two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, what do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: --remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I did-- because my brother wrote me a letter, a couple-- I don't know, a couple months ago from Israel. He asked me something that I wrote him, that he say, how could you remember? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In this time, I was a child. He was very little. I don't know nothing about. Oh, but you wasn't too much older than I am. And you remember this? How could you? And I don't know how to tell him, you know. Do you remember if you had any friends who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had little friends to-- yeah, we had in the business, we also had a lot of friends--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know. We was a, a big corporation in, in Lithuania. We were doing a lot of business with all kinds, with the government, you know. We used to make coop for the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when you were a boy, you had talked about that you were in the Jewish Boy Scouts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was when I was going even in the-- the gymnasium school. you know. So in spare time, we used to come in, you know, and they used to teach us how to help people, you know. And we used to go out on outing, you know, like in the woods sometime for camping, you know, stuff like that. And they used to always teach us how to help people, you know, older people and young people, you know, in case of disaster. This is a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Green.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get badges like the boys do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they used to give badges, too. I don't know if I had a badge [? this time. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] How many--

speaker: interviewer
text: --kids were in the troop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was quite a few. I do not remember how many, you know. If I would tell you, I have to just guess it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. So did you have a best friend, do you remember, a close friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had quite a few, you know, from Jewish kids, you know. We had a-- we used to always-- together, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we used to spend time a lot in school, too, you know, in spare time and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- and the school used to put up shows, you know? And the kids from school-- the students used to be the actors, you know. And they used to play music and all kind of stuff, you know, to entertain, you know, the-- Mhm. Did you ever get to be an actor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I, I wasn't in this. One time, they took me in a play where I didn't do nothing there, like a storekeeper. They made like a little store, you know? They was on the stage, and I like a-- like a storekeeper. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and what about grandparents? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- I, I have no idea because when I grew up, I didn't have my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were gone. How about uncles, aunts? Do you remember them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncles-- we had uncles in a different state, next to it, Latvia, if you heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You heard Latvia. There used to be a city, Dvinsk, because before the war, my father was in this city. My father married in, in Dvinsk. His wife is from Dvinsk. And he was living in Dvinsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: His wife, your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: See. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Since the uncles-- all of them was there. And it's-- years ago, when you used to go from one country to another one, you had to have a passport, like you go from here. It was difficult, you know? Well, my father used to go once in a while. Once in a year, I think, he used to go to his rabbi, you know. See, like in United State, they got a rabbi, you know, where they-- he's leader, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before holiday, he used to go. He used to bless them-- Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know [LAUGHS] And he used to bring the blessing to the family. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- what's your earliest memory of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the war? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The war started in Poland and Germany. And we was on the border. But I think this got nothing to do with Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, then let me take it back a little bit farther. When-- what was your earliest sense that there was going to be a problem, especially for the Jews, in Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the Russian took over Lithuania. It's the same like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometime a storm comes in here. So they know a couple-odd hours before. We didn't know nothing. What happened-- how they took over Lithuania is the Russian, Stalin's government, called in the Secretary of State of Lithuania when he was in Moscow. And in the same time when he was there, the tanks from Russia crossed the border. It's next to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they occupied Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia in a few hours, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before that, you had no inkling that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know they will come in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --see. We, we didn't know this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're saying that-- now, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940. Maybe we had an idea because, you see, when the war started, Poland and Germany-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so after the First World War, they proclaimed Lithuania a country. And the capital was Vilna for hundreds or thousands of years. But after the war, when they proclaimed Poland and all the countries independent-- and Lithuania, it's a general from Poland, without saying to nobody, he just-- in with his army when he's occupied Vilna, the capital. So Lithuania didn't have a capital for a long time, for 25 years, maybe, 28 years. They didn't have diplomatic relation. You couldn't send even a letter, even it's next door because they was in a rift, you know. After this, Poland gave ultimatum to Lithuania. After 28 years, if you don't make diplomatic relation with us, we will occupy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made it. They couldn't fight 2 million against 45 million. When the war started, Hitler with Stalin made the agreement they will split Poland. So Russia took off a big part of Poland-- Ukraine, they call this. And they took over Vilna.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they took over Vilna, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they called up the Lithuanian government. And they say, you didn't have Vilna never. And we got now Vilna. We like to give you a present-- Vilna. And you can come in and take over. We'll give you over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Lithuania mobilized all the recruits and the ex-soldiers, you know, just to take it over without war, the, the city. It was five miles from, from, from the border where they are now. They come over. The Russian hold them for three months near the border, and they didn't let them in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their excuse is they didn't say nobody because they cleaned out the city Vilna. They took out, out even the knobs from the doors. When the city was cleared, then they let in the-- [LAUGHS] --the army, the Lithuanian army to take it over. Then they took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, but after they took it over, they made bases there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --see? They made bases. Lithuania couldn't refuse nothing because you cannot fight Russia. Russia is 350 million people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians made bases there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Russians made bases, and they was there. So we had-- in Panevezys, we had a rabbi. Oh, but this rabbi was a elected member of the Lithuanian parliament even, see? And he wasn't too much in the city Panevezys. He was most in Africa or in the United State. And he used to go between the Jewish population and collect money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with the money he used to send over in Panevezys in the city to hold on the-- all the Jewish institutions-- a hospital and old-age homes and orphans' homes-- to supply money to cover the expense, you know. For holiday, they used to just come in back to the city, you know. Oh, but this was good for the city. Well, one time-- and in Lithuania, it's like this. A rabbi is not like here. Each synagogue got a rabbi. Each temple got a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the rabbi of all the city. There was 16 synagogues there. And he was the rabbi. And he was in his synagogue. He didn't go all of them, you know. But one time when he come over, and they'd made the bases there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the Russian. So he made a speech in, in synagogue to the people. And he say, today, he say, Russia gave Vilna to Lithuania. But in a few days later, in a short while, for interest, United States will have to pay with the whole country. They will take the whole country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he--
